## Apolipoprotein E, Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase (MTHFR) Mutation Risk of Senile Dementia

and

Midori Nishiyama 1, Yukie Kato 2, Michiyo Hashimoto 1, Satoru Yukawa 1, and Kenichi Omori 3

disease

We examined apolipoprotein E (Apo E) polymorphism and methylenetetrahydrofolate (MTHFR) 677 C to T mutation by using the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) method elderly Japanese aged 60 or more, and assessed whether these genetic factors with an increased risk for the clinical phenotypes of senile dementia, Alzheimer's and vascular dementia (VD) by cross-sectional survey.

the group

It was found that the Apo E * 4 allele were associated with an increased prevalence previously reported. Although, it was not strongly related to the severity of senile weak associaton between the ApoE genotype and the severity of dementia was suggested. proportion of patients with senile dementia was higher in the group of carriers mutation than in the group of noncarriers. Furthermore, the proportion of male senile dementia was higher in the group of homozygous for the mutation (+/+) than without the mutation (-/-). Notably in VD patients, 5 of 7 males had the +/+ genotype.

The results suggest that the ApoE * 4 genotype and the MTHFR mutation are associated the clinical phenotype and the clinical onset of senile dementia.

J Epidemiol, 2000 ; 10 : 163-172

vascular dementia, Alzheimer's disease, apolipoprotein E, methylenetetrahydrofolate polymerase chain reaction (PCR)

## INTRODUCTION

In Japan the proportion of elderly people over 65 years old is about 16% and increasing. The prevalence of senile dementia among the total population aged 65 or more in 1995 was about 6.9% 1), which represents a social problem. Vascular dementia (VD) is the most common form of senile dementia in Japan, with Alzheimer's disease (AD) next. Individuals either or both of these diseases account for at least 80% of patients with senile dementia 1). The cause brovascular atherosclerosis, whereas the cause of AD remains definitively unknown. However, recent molecular tions have elucidated the genetic factors that play a significant role in the development of AD and vascular atherosclerosis zs) A close relation between apolipoprotein-E (apoE) alleles and with reductase,

Received June 23, 1999 ; accepted October

affected with of VD is cereinvestigaAD has been reported by many investigators 4-6). It has als been reported that the C to T transition of 5, 10 Methylentetrahydofolate reductase (MRTHFR) gene at nucleotide position 677 results in a decrease of enzymatic activity and increase of the plasma total homocysteine leve and that hyperhomocysteinemia is a risk factor for atheroscle otic cerebrovascular and coronary heart diseases 7,8). Since association of atherosclerosis with senile dementia is worthy consideration in not only VD but also AD, we examined both the apoE polymorphism and the MTHFR mutation in elderly people and assessed whether these genetic factors increase t risk for and modify the clinical phenotype of AD and VD .

## MATERIALS AND METHODS

the DNA Extractor WB Kit (Sodium iodide method, Wako).

We investigated 100 elderly persons (25 men and 75 women) over 60 years old (average age: 76.8 ± 9.26SD), who comprised residents of a special nursing home for the aged and the patients who were referred to the two hospitals for senile dementia or other diseases. Consent for participation in the study was provided by the subjects themselves or their legal guardians. Dementia was defined using the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Third Edition, Revised (DSM-III-R) 9) referring to the diagnosis Tomography (CT) or Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). The grade of severity of dementia was diagnosed with the Hughes' clinical dementia rating (CDR). Among the subjects, 33 (average age: 73.0±9.90SD) were diagnosed as not having senile dementia and the residual 67 (average age: 78.6± 8.40SD) were diagnosed as senile dementia. 35 patients had vascular dementia (VD, average age: 81.3 Alzheimer's disease (AD, average age: 75.7 residual eight corresponded to the mixed type of senile dementia (average age: 75.5±9.96SD). With informed consent for genetic examination, we took venous blood samples from all subjects and stored them separately at-80ーC in O.5-ml aliquots until the DNA analysis. Leukocyte DNA was obtained

of Computed 6.41 SD), 24 had 9.37SD), and the using For isotyping of apolipoprotein E, leukocyte DNA samples were amplified by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) in a DNA thermal Cycler (Perkin Elmer) using oligonucleotide primers F4 (5'-ACAGAATTCGCCCCGGCCTGGTACAC3') and F6 (5'-TAAGCTTGGCACGGGCTGTCCAAGGA-3') as previously described 10, 11). Each amplification reaction contained about 0.8〜1 μ1 of leukocyte DNA, 17.2 pmol of each primer, 10% dimethyl sulfoxide, and 1.5 units of Amplitag DNA polymerise (Perkin Elmer) with PCR buffer and dNTP mixture in a final volume of 30,μ 1. initial melting temperature The PCR reaction had an of 95ーC(5 min.) followed by 30 cycles of melting(95°C,1min.), annealing (60°C,1 min), extension(70°C,2min.), and denaturation (70°C,10 min.). After PCR amplication, 5 units of Hhal (Wako, Nippon gene) was added directly to each reaction mixture for digestion of t apoE sequences(>3 hours at 37ーC). Each digestion product was loaded onto 10% polyacrylamide gel (15l×15cm,1-mm thick), electrophoresed at 30 mA for 1 hour and 20 minutes, stained with ethidium bromide, and visualized by UV light. HhaI cut the PCR products of ε 3 to generate 91-bp and 48-bp fragments. Fragments of 72-bp and 48-bp are produced and fragments of 91-bp and 83-bp are produced inε4, in ε 2. <!-- image -->

(Fig.1).

To detect the mutant allele of Methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR), the same leukocyte DNA samples were examined. The PCR procedure was basically the same with for the isotyping of apoE, but the primers used for the analysis were 5'-TGAAGGAGAAGGTGTCTGCGGGA-3' and 5'AGGACGGTGCGGTGAGAGTG-3', and the restriction enzyme Hinfl was used to identify the mutation, as described by Frosst et al. 75. These primers generate a fragment of 198bp. The substitution creates a Hinfl recognition sequence which digests the 198-bp fragment into 175- and 23-bp fragments. As the latter fragment has been run off, the existence of a 175-bp fragment indicates the mutant allele. The mutant allele was designated as "+", and the wild-type that means absence of mutant allele was designated as "- ". The three possible genotypes are +/+homozygosity, +/-heterozygosity, and -/ - homozygosity (Fig.2).

All the subjects were classified into Apo E e 4-carriers or non-carriers. According to the three types of MTHFR genotypes, they were divided into "+/+", "+/-" ' - / -" . Each group was examined by the proportion and

the composition of senile dementia, average age, average ag of onset, severity of dementia (CDR) and the rate of decline dementia (CDR/years from onset of dementia). The difference in these information between the different genotypes of apoE and MTHFR were statistically analyzed with the Yates-corrected chi-square test, Welch's t-test, Pearson's correlation ficient, and the multiple regression analyses using the compu er software HALBAU (N88-Japanese BASIC) and Statistical Package for Social Sciences (SPSS, 7.5J).

The prevalence of dementia was higher in the carriers (23/27) than in the noncarriers The difference was remarkable especially in the prevalence AD (p<0.001). Table 3 shows the average age, average age of onset and the severity of senile dementia in E 4 carriers and noncarriers. The average age of the E 4 carriers in non-dementia was higher than that of noncarriers (p=0.05). In respect the grade of severity of dementia, the average Hughes' clinical dementia rating (CDR) was higher in the carriers noncarriers (p=0.07). We also examined the CDR per year from onset as the rate of decline. The average CDR per year from onset was lower in the noncarriers (p=0.09). These results suggest that there is also an association between Apo E E 4 and severity of senile dementia.

The correlation matrix among Apo E, MTHFR subtypes and other variables was obtain only dementia patients (n=67) and is presented in Table 9. Apo E has positive relationships with AD as well as the decline of severity of dementia (CDR/year from onset), while MTHFR +/+ genotype was found to be correlated with sex of patients.

Table 10 shows the results of logistic regression analyses by each subtypes of dementia. It revealed that Apo E E 4 was a predictive factor for dementia patients (p<0.05). Specifically, Apo E E 4 was a strong predictor for the onset of AD from this analysis (p=0.0001). On the other hand, the age of subject was the only factor associated with VD, and the VD was seen more in older patients (p=0.001).

Table 10 shows the results of logistic regression analyses by each subtypes of dementia. It revealed that Apo E E 4 was a predictive factor for dementia patients (p<0.05). Specifically, Apo E E 4 was a strong predictor for the onset of AD from this analysis (p=0.0001). On the other hand, the age of subject was the only factor associated with VD, and the VD was seen more in older patients (p=0.001).

## DISCUSSION

Alzheimer disease (AD) and vascular dementia (VD), two major causes of senile dementia, have imposed a major burde on modem public health care. AD and VD, with the exception of very few familial entities with Mendelian inheritance, regarded as multifactorial genetic disorders. Molecular inv gations using the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) method have analyzed their genetic background. These approaches may be effective for the exploration of preventive methods senile dementia.

Further, age, sex, and Apo E status were used to investigate the relationship with +/+ MTHFR homozygous (Table 11). Male subjects with any subtypes of dementia were related with MTHFR (p<0.005). Interestingly, this relationship did not exist

In our study, the Apo E E 4 allele was associated with increased risk for AD, as currently suggested. According Forno et al., the Apo E genotype does not strongly influe the rate of decline in AD 13). Our study suggested that the E * 4 allele worsens the severity of senile dementia. effects of E 4 need to be further investigated. Apolipopr with different

In present study, the frequencies of MTHFR mutation in nondementia were similar to those previously reported. Th MTHFR mutation was initially proposed to be associated with the development of coronary artery disease 19). However, th proposal is contraversial among some reseachers According to Catteneo et al., the homozygous MTHFR gene mutation is not a risk factor for deep-vein thrombosis increases the risk associated with factor V, a common risk tor for deep-vein thrombosis 21). A study by Rees et al. sugg ed that neither factor V nor thermolabile MTHFR were risk factors for premature death among the very elderly 22).

E polymorphism was originally studied as a risk factor erosclerosis of heart disease 14,15) Apo E * 2, one of three common alleles of the Apo E isoforms termed * 2, * was elucidated to be relate with atherosclerosis in patients with type III hyperlipidemia. Apo E plays in the transport of cholesterol from peripheral tissues liver; thus the modification of lipid metabolism in Apo morphism can account for its relation to an increased atherosclerosis. Apo E * 4 is known to increase the total cholesterol and betalipoprotein 16), which for atherosclerosis and eventually the development However, the mechanism by which Apo E E 4 operates provoke the onset and worsen the clinical symptoms remains unknown. Atherosclerosis may partially the development and phenotypic modifications of AD. a study in Rotterdam suggested that both AD and VD associated with atherosclerosis and that there was tion between Apo E and atherosclerosis in the etiology

The interrelation of Apo E E 4 to other risk factors also remains unclarified. Mutations of the very low lipoproptein receptor gene, encoding one of the Apo tors, were reported to have a genetic association with AD I8). This would also suggest that impaired lipid metabolism contributes to the onset of AD.

Severe MTHFR deficiency gives rise to homocystinuria, which is characterized by neurological abnormalities mature atherosclerotic changes with thromboembolism. thermolabile variant of MTHFR does not create homocystinemia and neurological abnormalities. The genotypes thermolabile variant mutation of MTHFR, comprising +/-, and -/-, lead to significantly different molability as described by Frosst et al. ~. It was the frequency of the +/+, +/-, and -/-genotype Japanese middle-aged males was 11, 54, and 35% 12), respec-

play a role in In fact, were an interacof AD i1~. for AD density E recepsporadic and preThe of the +1+, enzyme therreported that in The possible association of the MTHFR mutation with vascular diseases led us to hypothesize that it is a candidat risk factor for VD and AD. A causal relation between th MTHFR mutation and the onset of AD and VD was suggested in our study and the grade of severity of dementia was pa doxically higher in the -/than +/+ group. There was strong association between the MTHFR mutation and senile dementia; nevertheless, the mutation was considered to mod fy the clinical phenotype of senile dementia. Perhaps the m remarkable result from our study was that the proportion male patients with senile dementia was higher in the gro homozygous for the mutation (+l+) than in the group with out the mutation (-/-). This suggests that the MTHFR muta tion is more influential in men than women in causing t onset of VD. In the Hisayama study by Yoshitake et.al., t incidence of VD was higher for men than for women in Japan 23). Deloughery et al. reported that the mean homocy teine levels increased by MTHFR homozygotes in the vascular disease subjects were significantly higher than in healthy matched subjects and it was slight more apparent for men. But there were no reports for the MTHFR genotypes by sex.

5. Poirier J, Davignon J, Bouthillier D, et al. Apolipop E polymorphism and Alzheimer's disease. Lancet, 1993; 342:697-699.
6. Saunders AM, Strittmatter WJ, Schmechel D, et al Association of apolipoprotein E alle r 4 with late-o familial and sporadic Alzheimer's disease. Neurology 1993; 43: 1467-1472.
7. Frosst P, Blom HJ, Milos R, et al. A candidate gene risk factor for vascular disease: a common mutation methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase, Nature Genet, 1995 10: 111-113.
8. Wilcken DEL, Wang XL, Sim AS, et al. Distribution in healthy and coronary population of the methylenetetrahy drofolate reductase (MTHFR) C677 T mutation : Arteriosclerosis, thrombosis, and Vascular Biology, 1996; 16,878-882.

Therefore, further epidemiological study is necessary to establish the role of the MTHFR mutation as a risk factor for senile dementia. Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the major cause of dementia in most western countries, whereas vascular dementia (VD) is more common in Japan. According to the Honolulu-Asia aging study, the prevalence of AD among elderly Japanese-American men in Hawaii was higher than that in elderly Japanese men, and was comparable to that in an European-ancestry population 25). These observations suggest that environmental factors play a major role in the onset and clinical variations of senile dementia in addition to genetic risk factors. It can be readily understood that vascular atherosclerosis and eventual VD are affected by life style such as alcohol consumption and smoking. The proposed relation of lipid metabolism to AD, as mentioned above, also implies the importance of life style in preventing the onset of AD.

11. Emi M, Wu LL, Robertoson MA, et al. Genotyping and sequence analysis of apolipoprotein E isoforms. Genomics, 1988; 3: 373-379.

Our study revealed that Apo E may be a risk factor and a phenotypic modification factor for senile dementia, MTHFR is slightly associated with the onset of senile dementia, especialy among men. These results suggest that cerebral atherosclerosis contributes to the development of not only VD, but also AD. Investigations on a larger number of senile dementia patients will elucidate the relation of these genetic factors to senile dementia in more detail. Through this research, a way to prevent senile dementia might be found.

## ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

This work was supported in part by research grants from Chiyoda Mutual Life Foundation. We would like to thank Drs . Rie Kiyomiya and Tatsuo Kawaguchi for their help in sample collection. We also thank Drs . Kimihiro Nakae, Gen Nishimura, and Masanori Takahashi at Dokkyo University School of Medicine, for their helpful suggestions on preparing the manuscript.
